nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of shallow lagoons and seaward reefs, typically at depths of 0–30 m, where the ranges of the two parent species overlap.

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting spirulina, marine algae (nori), high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp, and frozen meaty foods. This hybrid tends to require a notably algae-rich diet, consistent with both parent species. Regular feeding 2–3 times daily is generally advised.

summary: The Lemonpeel Hybrid Angelfish is a rare natural cross between the Lemonpeel Angelfish (Centropyge flavissima) and the Halfblack Angelfish (Centropyge vroliki), displaying variable coloration that blends the yellow of one parent with the dark posterior markings of the other. Generally considered reef-safe with caution.
careNotes:
The Lemonpeel Hybrid Angelfish is a naturally occurring interspecific hybrid — meaning a cross between two distinct species in the wild — between Centropyge flavissima, the Lemonpeel Angelfish, and Centropyge vroliki, the Halfblack or Pearlscale Angelfish. It is found in areas where the ranges of both parent species overlap, particularly around the Marshall Islands. Because it arises from two parent species rather than being a recognized species in its own right, coloration is variable and intermediate between the two: most individuals show a partially yellow body with some of the dark posterior pigmentation characteristic of C. vroliki. The hybrid is commonly described as a rare find in the aquarium trade.

Care requirements closely follow those of both parent species. A well-established aquarium with abundant live rock is generally advised, as this gives the fish ample surface area to graze naturally on algae. Like both parent species, this hybrid tends to nip at small-polyped stony corals and clam mantles, so it is best described as reef-safe with caution rather than fully reef-safe. It is best kept singly; introducing other dwarf angelfish of the same genus or similarly colored fish tends to result in aggression. Like its parent species, this hybrid is expected to be a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning it begins life as female and may transition to male — and in the wild the parent species typically form harems, with one dominant male living alongside several females.

A minimum aquarium volume of around 300 liters is generally recommended, with a temperature range of 24–27.5°C targeted for routine keeping.
breedingNotes: No captive breeding reports have been confirmed for this hybrid. As a cross between two Centropyge species, it is expected to follow the general pattern of the genus: protogynous hermaphroditism (beginning life as female, with the possibility of transitioning to male) and broadcast spawning, in which eggs and sperm are released into the water column to fertilize freely. Wild hybridization between the parent species has been documented in areas where their ranges overlap. Captive breeding of either parent species is already regarded as advanced; breeding the hybrid is likely equally or more challenging, and has not been achieved outside specialist facilities.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for parent-species cross-verification. FishBase records C. flavissima at max 14 cm TL, temperature 24–28°C, family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes. No FishBase entry exists for the hybrid itself.

additional.name: FishBase (C. vroliki)
additional.notes: Used for parent-species cross-verification. FishBase records C. vroliki (= C. vrolikii) at max 12 cm TL, temperature 25.6–29.3°C, native range Western Pacific including Marshall Islands. Confirms hybrid origin zone.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia (C. flavissima)
additional.notes: Third source for parent-species parameters. Cites max 14 cm, optimal temp 27°C, minimum tank 300 L. Used to triangulate temperature and tank size for the hybrid entry.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding reports confirmed for this interspecific hybrid. Expected to follow parent-species pattern: protogynous hermaphrodite, broadcast spawner. Captive rearing is likely advanced to not-achievable outside specialist facilities.
